questions.q: How many satellites did the record-setting Indian mission launch in a single flight in 2017?
questions.explanation: On 15 February 2017 ISRO launched a record 104 satellites in a single mission.

questions.q: Which Indian space agency carried out the 104-satellite launch?
questions.explanation: The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) conducted the mission.

questions.q: Which family of rockets was used to launch the 104 satellites?
questions.explanation: The mission used the Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle (PSLV).

questions.q: In what year did the 104-satellite single-mission launch take place?
questions.explanation: The launch occurred in February 2017.

questions.q: What did the 104-satellite launch set at the time it was achieved?
questions.explanation: It set a world record for the most satellites launched in a single mission.

questions.q: From which Indian spaceport was the 104-satellite mission launched?
questions.explanation: It was launched from the Satish Dhawan Space Centre at Sriharikota.

questions.q: What does the 'P' in PSLV stand for?
questions.explanation: PSLV stands for Polar Satellite Launch Vehicle.

questions.q: The 104 satellites were placed into what kind of orbit?
questions.explanation: The satellites were injected into a sun-synchronous polar orbit, typical for PSLV missions.

questions.q: What was the primary Indian satellite carried on the 104-satellite mission?
questions.explanation: The main payload was a Cartosat-2 series Earth-observation satellite.

questions.q: Most of the 104 satellites belonged to which category?
questions.explanation: The vast majority were small nanosatellites from foreign customers.

questions.q: What was the specific designation of the PSLV flight that launched the 104 satellites?
questions.explanation: The mission was flight PSLV-C37.

questions.q: Which previous record did ISRO's 104-satellite launch surpass?
questions.explanation: It beat the earlier record of 37 satellites set by a Russian Dnepr launch in 2014.

questions.q: How many of the 104 satellites were from the United States?
questions.explanation: Of the 104 satellites, 96 belonged to U.S. companies, the largest national share.

questions.q: A large block of the foreign nanosatellites came from which US company's Earth-imaging constellation?
questions.explanation: The mission carried 88 'Dove' satellites for Planet (then Planet Labs).

questions.q: Which PSLV configuration variant flew the PSLV-C37 mission?
questions.explanation: PSLV-C37 flew in the powerful PSLV-XL configuration with six strap-on boosters.

questions.q: Who was the Chairman of ISRO at the time of the 104-satellite launch in 2017?
questions.explanation: A. S. Kiran Kumar was ISRO Chairman in February 2017.

questions.q: Including the primary satellite, the 104 total comprised how many co-passenger satellites?
questions.explanation: There was 1 primary Cartosat-2 series satellite plus 103 co-passenger satellites.

questions.q: Two of the 103 co-passengers were ISRO's own nanosatellites named what?
questions.explanation: ISRO included its INS-1A and INS-1B Indian Nano Satellites on the flight.

questions.q: Why is releasing 104 satellites from one rocket technically challenging?
options: The satellites must be deployed without colliding with each other
The rocket must reach the Moon
Each satellite needs its own crew
questions.explanation: Deploying so many satellites in quick succession requires precise sequencing and velocity changes to avoid collisions.

questions.q: The Cartosat-2 series satellite on PSLV-C37 is primarily used for what purpose?
questions.explanation: Cartosat-2 series satellites provide high-resolution imagery for Earth observation and mapping.

questions.q: ISRO's commercial arm that arranged the foreign satellite launches at the time was known as what?
questions.explanation: Antrix Corporation handled ISRO's commercial launch contracts in 2017.

questions.q: Roughly how long did the deployment of all 104 satellites take after the rocket reached orbit?
questions.explanation: After reaching orbit, all 104 satellites separated from the PSLV fourth stage in rapid sequence over about 12 minutes.

questions.q: The 104-satellite world record was later surpassed in 2021 by a launch from which company?
questions.explanation: SpaceX's Transporter-1 mission in January 2021 deployed 143 satellites, breaking ISRO's record.

questions.q: On what exact date was the PSLV-C37 104-satellite mission launched?
questions.explanation: PSLV-C37 lifted off on 15 February 2017.

questions.q: Besides the US and India, which countries had satellites on the PSLV-C37 flight?
options: Netherlands, Switzerland, Israel, Kazakhstan and UAE
questions.explanation: Customer satellites also came from the Netherlands, Switzerland, Israel, Kazakhstan and the UAE.

questions.q: The PSLV is notable for which fuel arrangement among its stages?
options: It alternates solid and liquid propellant stages
It uses only solid propellant throughout
questions.explanation: The PSLV is a four-stage rocket that alternates between solid and liquid propulsion stages.

questions.q: Into approximately what altitude orbit were the PSLV-C37 satellites primarily injected?
questions.explanation: The satellites were placed into a sun-synchronous polar orbit at roughly 505 km altitude.

questions.q: Why did ISRO use the PSLV rather than the GSLV for the 104-satellite mission?
options: PSLV is well suited for small satellites in polar/sun-synchronous orbits
PSLV is the only Indian rocket
questions.explanation: The PSLV is ISRO's reliable workhorse for placing small and medium satellites into polar and sun-synchronous orbits.

questions.q: What feature of the PSLV's upper stage made deploying 104 satellites in sequence feasible?
options: Its restartable liquid-fueled fourth stage allowing controlled deployments
questions.explanation: The PSLV's liquid-fueled fourth stage provided the controlled platform from which satellites were sequentially ejected.

questions.q: The 104-satellite mission reinforced ISRO's reputation in what global market?
questions.explanation: The mission strengthened ISRO's standing as a cost-effective provider of commercial small-satellite launches.
